questions.question: What is the default OSPF hello interval on a broadcast network?
questions.explanation: On broadcast and point-to-point networks, OSPF sends hello packets every 10 seconds. On NBMA networks, the default is 30 seconds.

questions.question: What is the default OSPF dead interval on a broadcast network?
questions.explanation: The dead interval is 4 times the hello interval. With a 10-second hello, the dead interval is 40 seconds.

questions.question: Which OSPF neighbor state indicates that the router has received a hello from a neighbor but the local router ID is not in the neighbor's hello?
questions.explanation: In the Init state, a hello has been received from a neighbor, but bidirectional communication has not yet been established because the local router's RID is not listed in the neighbor's hello packet.

questions.question: In which OSPF state does DR/BDR election occur?
questions.explanation: DR/BDR election happens at the 2-Way state. Once all routers on a segment reach 2-Way, the election takes place before moving to ExStart.

questions.question: What is the default OSPF cost for a FastEthernet interface with the default reference bandwidth?
questions.explanation: OSPF cost = Reference BW / Interface BW. Default reference is 100 Mbps. 100/100 = 1. This is why Cisco recommends changing the reference bandwidth.

questions.question: Which command changes the OSPF reference bandwidth to 1 Gbps?
questions.explanation: The auto-cost reference-bandwidth command is entered under the OSPF router process and sets the reference bandwidth in Mbps.

questions.question: What determines the OSPF Router ID if no router-id is manually configured and no loopback interfaces exist?
options: The lowest IP on any active interface
The highest IP on any active interface
questions.explanation: OSPF selects the Router ID in this order: 1) manual router-id, 2) highest loopback IP, 3) highest IP on any active physical interface.

questions.question: Two OSPF routers on the same broadcast segment cannot form an adjacency. Both show Init state. What is the most likely cause?
questions.explanation: If routers are stuck in Init, they can send but not receive each other's hellos. An ACL blocking OSPF multicast (224.0.0.5) on one side is a common cause. Mismatched timers would prevent even Init.

questions.question: Which OSPF network type does NOT elect a DR/BDR?
questions.explanation: Point-to-point networks have only two routers, so there is no need for DR/BDR election. Broadcast and NBMA networks elect DR/BDR.

questions.question: What command enables OSPF process 1 on a router?
questions.explanation: The router ospf <process-id> command enters OSPF configuration mode. The process ID is locally significant.

questions.question: What happens when an OSPF router with priority 0 is on a broadcast segment?
options: It cannot become DR or BDR
questions.explanation: Setting OSPF priority to 0 means the router will never become DR or BDR. It will remain a DROther.

questions.question: OSPF routers are stuck in ExStart/Exchange state. What is the most likely cause?
questions.explanation: ExStart/Exchange stuck state is almost always caused by MTU mismatch. The routers cannot agree on DBD packet sizes during database synchronization.

questions.question: Which command shows OSPF neighbor adjacencies and their states?
questions.explanation: show ip ospf neighbor displays all OSPF neighbors with their states, priorities, dead times, and interface information.

questions.question: What is the OSPF multicast address used by all OSPF routers?
questions.explanation: 224.0.0.5 is AllSPFRouters, used by all OSPF routers. 224.0.0.6 is AllDRouters, used to communicate with DR/BDR only.

questions.question: A network admin sees that two routers have the same OSPF Router ID. What will happen?
options: The second router will auto-change its RID
Routing tables will be corrupted on both routers
Neighbor adjacency will fail or flap
questions.explanation: Duplicate Router IDs prevent stable OSPF adjacency formation. The routers may form then drop the adjacency repeatedly, causing route flapping.

questions.question: Which OSPF packet type is used to request specific LSAs from a neighbor?
questions.explanation: Link State Request (LSR) packets are sent to request specific LSAs that were seen in DBD packets but are newer than the local copy.

questions.question: How does OSPF determine the best path to a destination?
questions.explanation: OSPF uses cumulative cost (sum of all interface costs along the path) to determine the best route. Lower cost = better path.

questions.question: What is the OSPF administrative distance?
questions.explanation: OSPF has a default administrative distance of 110. This is higher than EIGRP (90) but lower than RIP (120) and IS-IS (115).

questions.question: Which command configures OSPF on an interface using the newer method?
questions.explanation: The ip ospf <process-id> area <area-id> command is configured directly on the interface. This is the newer, preferred method over the network command.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the OSPF network command wildcard mask?
options: To define the subnet mask for the network
To match which interfaces will participate in OSPF
To set the OSPF cost on matching interfaces
To filter routes advertised by OSPF
questions.explanation: The wildcard mask in the OSPF network command is used to match interface IP addresses. Interfaces with IPs matching the network/wildcard combination will be enabled for OSPF in the specified area.

questions.question: On which type of network does OSPF form full adjacencies with all neighbors (not just DR/BDR)?
questions.explanation: On point-to-point networks, all OSPF neighbors form full adjacencies since there are only two routers and no DR/BDR election.

questions.question: What command makes OSPF advertise a default route to other OSPF routers?
questions.explanation: The default-information originate command under OSPF router config advertises a default route into OSPF. The router must have a default route in its table unless you add the 'always' keyword.

questions.question: What does OSPF use to uniquely identify each router in the OSPF domain?
questions.explanation: The Router ID is a 32-bit value that uniquely identifies each OSPF router. It looks like an IP address but is just an identifier.

questions.question: After changing the OSPF Router ID, what must you do for it to take effect?
options: Reload the router or clear the OSPF process
questions.explanation: Changing the router-id command does not take effect until the OSPF process is restarted with 'clear ip ospf process' or the router is reloaded.

questions.question: What type of LSA does an ABR generate to advertise inter-area routes?
questions.explanation: ABRs generate Type 3 Summary LSAs to advertise routes between areas. Type 1 are router LSAs, Type 2 are network LSAs from DR, and Type 5 are external from ASBRs.

questions.question: Which OSPF router generates Type 2 (Network) LSAs?
questions.explanation: Only the Designated Router (DR) generates Type 2 Network LSAs for multi-access segments. These describe all routers connected to the segment.

questions.question: What command sets the OSPF interface priority?
questions.explanation: The ip ospf priority command is configured on the interface to influence DR/BDR election. Valid range is 0-255.

questions.question: A router running OSPF has these neighbors: R1=FULL/DR, R2=2WAY/DROTHER, R3=FULL/BDR. What role does this router have?
questions.explanation: DROthers form FULL adjacencies only with DR and BDR, and remain in 2-WAY with other DROthers. Since this router is FULL with DR and BDR but 2-WAY with a DROTHER, it is a DROther.

questions.question: Which OSPF state means the LSDB is fully synchronized between neighbors?
questions.explanation: The Full state indicates complete LSDB synchronization between OSPF neighbors. This is the final state of a successful adjacency.

questions.question: What happens if OSPF hello and dead timers don't match between neighbors?
options: Adjacency forms but routes are not exchanged
Adjacency forms with the lower timer
The router with higher timers becomes DR
questions.explanation: Mismatched hello and dead timers prevent OSPF adjacency formation. Both sides must agree on these timers for the neighbor relationship to establish.

questions.question: What is the purpose of HSRP?
questions.explanation: HSRP (Hot Standby Router Protocol) provides first-hop gateway redundancy by allowing multiple routers to share a virtual IP and MAC address.

questions.question: What is the default HSRP priority?
questions.explanation: The default HSRP priority is 100. The router with the highest priority becomes the Active router. Ties are broken by highest IP address.

questions.question: In HSRP, what does the preempt command do?
options: Forces the router to become active immediately
Allows a higher-priority router to take over as Active
Prevents the standby router from becoming active
questions.explanation: Preempt allows a router with a higher priority to take over the Active role from the current Active router. Without preempt, the current Active keeps its role even if a higher-priority router joins.

questions.question: What virtual MAC address does HSRP version 1 use for group 1?
questions.explanation: HSRP v1 uses the MAC format 0000.0c07.acXX where XX is the group number in hex. Group 1 = 0000.0c07.ac01.

questions.question: Which protocol is Cisco proprietary for first hop redundancy?
questions.explanation: HSRP is Cisco proprietary. VRRP is the open standard equivalent. GLBP is also Cisco but adds load balancing.

questions.question: What HSRP state is the router in when it is actively forwarding traffic for the virtual IP?
questions.explanation: The Active router is responsible for forwarding traffic sent to the virtual IP address. The Standby router is the backup ready to take over.

questions.question: How do you configure HSRP on an interface?
questions.explanation: HSRP is configured with the 'standby' command on the interface, specifying the group number and virtual IP address.

questions.question: A network has two HSRP routers. R1 has priority 110 with preempt. R2 has priority 100 and is currently Active. What happens when R1 comes online?
options: R2 remains Active, R1 becomes Standby
R1 becomes Active, R2 becomes Standby
questions.explanation: Because R1 has a higher priority (110) AND preempt is configured, R1 will take over the Active role from R2 when it comes online.

questions.question: What is the default hello interval for HSRP?
questions.explanation: HSRP sends hello packets every 3 seconds by default. The hold timer is 10 seconds (roughly 3x the hello).

questions.question: What key difference does GLBP have over HSRP?
options: GLBP provides load balancing across multiple routers
questions.explanation: GLBP (Gateway Load Balancing Protocol) distributes traffic across multiple routers using multiple virtual MAC addresses, providing both redundancy and load balancing.

questions.question: What is the command to configure a default static route?
questions.explanation: A default static route uses 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 as the destination and mask, pointing to the next-hop IP. This matches all destinations not in the routing table.

questions.question: What is a floating static route?
options: A route that uses DHCP for next-hop
A backup static route with a higher AD than the primary route
A route that automatically adjusts its metric
A route to a loopback interface
questions.explanation: A floating static route has a manually set administrative distance higher than the primary routing protocol. It only appears in the routing table when the primary route fails.

questions.question: What is the administrative distance of a static route by default?
questions.explanation: Static routes have a default AD of 1 (0 is for directly connected networks). This makes static routes preferred over any dynamic routing protocol.

questions.question: Which command creates a static route to network 192.168.10.0/24 via next-hop 10.0.0.2?
questions.explanation: The ip route command uses the format: ip route <network> <mask> <next-hop-ip>. CIDR notation is not used in Cisco IOS static routes.

questions.question: What type of static route specifies the exit interface instead of a next-hop IP?
questions.explanation: A directly attached (or directly connected) static route specifies the exit interface. The router ARPs for the destination on that interface.

questions.question: What is a host route?
options: A route to a single IP address with /32 mask
A route learned from the host
A route to the default gateway
A route to a directly connected network
questions.explanation: A host route has a /32 subnet mask (255.255.255.255), matching exactly one IP address. It's used when you need to route traffic to a specific host.

questions.question: What is the IPv6 static route command for a default route via next-hop 2001:db8::1?
questions.explanation: The IPv6 default route uses ::/0 as the destination prefix. The command starts with 'ipv6 route' not 'ip route'.

questions.question: What does 'S*' mean in the routing table output?
questions.explanation: In show ip route, S* indicates a static candidate default route. The asterisk means it is a candidate for the gateway of last resort.

questions.question: A router has both a static route (AD 1) and an OSPF route (AD 110) to the same network. Which is used?
options: Both are used for load balancing
The route with the lowest metric
questions.explanation: The route with the lowest Administrative Distance is preferred. Static (AD 1) beats OSPF (AD 110), so the static route is installed in the routing table.

questions.question: How do you configure a floating static route as backup to OSPF?
questions.explanation: Set the AD higher than OSPF (110), like 111, at the end of the ip route command. The static route only enters the table if the OSPF route disappears.

questions.question: What command verifies that a static route is in the routing table?
questions.explanation: show ip route static filters the routing table to show only static routes. show running-config | include ip route shows configured static routes. Both are useful verification methods.

questions.question: What is the next-hop type called when you specify both exit interface and next-hop IP?
questions.explanation: A fully specified static route includes both the exit interface and the next-hop IP address, which is the most explicit and recommended form.

questions.question: What does 'O' represent in the routing table?
questions.explanation: In the routing table, O indicates an OSPF-learned route. C=Connected, S=Static, D=EIGRP, R=RIP, B=BGP.

questions.question: What does 'via' mean in a routing table entry?
options: The next-hop IP address to reach the destination
questions.explanation: The 'via' field in a routing table entry indicates the next-hop IP address that packets should be forwarded to reach the destination network.

questions.question: What is the gateway of last resort?
options: The route used when no other route matches
questions.explanation: The gateway of last resort is the default route (0.0.0.0/0). It is used when no more specific route matches the destination IP address.

questions.question: If a router has routes for 10.0.0.0/8, 10.1.0.0/16, and 10.1.1.0/24, which is used for a packet to 10.1.1.50?
questions.explanation: Longest prefix match: the router selects the route with the most specific (longest) matching prefix. /24 is more specific than /16 or /8 for 10.1.1.50.

questions.question: What is the longest prefix match rule?
options: The route with the longest network prefix wins
The route with the lowest metric wins
The route with the lowest AD wins
questions.explanation: Longest prefix match is the primary rule routers use for forwarding decisions. The most specific route (longest matching prefix) is always selected first, before AD or metric.

questions.question: A router has a static route (AD 1) to 10.0.0.0/8 and an OSPF route to 10.0.0.0/16. A packet arrives for 10.0.0.5. Which route is used?
questions.explanation: Longest prefix match takes priority over Administrative Distance. The /16 OSPF route is more specific than the /8 static route for 10.0.0.5.

questions.question: What does [110/20] mean next to a route in 'show ip route'?
questions.explanation: The first number in brackets is the Administrative Distance and the second is the metric (cost). [110/20] means AD 110 (OSPF) with a metric of 20.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of Spanning Tree Protocol?
questions.explanation: STP prevents Layer 2 loops in networks with redundant paths by blocking some ports while keeping others forwarding.

questions.question: What value determines which switch becomes the root bridge?
questions.explanation: The switch with the lowest Bridge ID (Priority + MAC) becomes the root bridge. Default priority is 32768.

questions.question: What is the default STP bridge priority?
questions.explanation: The default bridge priority is 32768. Priority must be set in increments of 4096 and ranges from 0 to 61440.

questions.question: How long does STP convergence take with default timers?
questions.explanation: STP convergence takes 30-50 seconds: Max Age (20s) + Listening (15s) + Learning (15s). This is why RSTP was developed.

questions.question: What are the STP port states in order?
questions.explanation: STP ports transition: Blocking → Listening (15s) → Learning (15s) → Forwarding. Listening sends/receives BPDUs, Learning adds MACs.

questions.question: What does PortFast do?
options: Immediately transitions a port to Forwarding
questions.explanation: PortFast skips the Listening and Learning states, transitioning directly to Forwarding. Only use on access ports connected to end devices.

questions.question: What happens when a PortFast-enabled port with BPDU Guard receives a BPDU?
options: The port goes to blocking state
The port is put in err-disabled state
questions.explanation: BPDU Guard places the port in err-disabled state when a BPDU is received, preventing potential loops from unauthorized switches.

questions.question: What is a root port?
options: The port on the root bridge that connects to other switches
The port on a non-root switch with the best path to the root bridge
The first port configured on a switch
questions.explanation: Each non-root switch has exactly one root port — the port with the lowest cost path to the root bridge.

questions.question: What is a designated port?
options: The port that connects to the root bridge
The port on a segment that forwards traffic toward the root
The port with the highest priority
questions.explanation: Each segment has one designated port — the port with the best path back to root on that segment. All root bridge ports are designated.

questions.question: Switch A (priority 32768, MAC 0000.0000.000A) and Switch B (priority 32768, MAC 0000.0000.000B) are connected. Which is root?
questions.explanation: With equal priorities, the switch with the lowest MAC address becomes root. 000A < 000B, so Switch A wins.

questions.question: Which RSTP port state replaces Blocking, Listening, and Disabled?
questions.explanation: RSTP consolidates Blocking, Listening, and Disabled into a single Discarding state. RSTP has only 3 states: Discarding, Learning, Forwarding.

questions.question: What is the STP path cost for a Gigabit Ethernet link?
questions.explanation: The IEEE revised STP costs: 10G=2, 1G=4, 100M=19, 10M=100. These were updated from the original short values.

questions.question: What command makes a switch become the root bridge?
questions.explanation: spanning-tree vlan <id> root primary sets the priority to a value low enough to guarantee root bridge election (usually 24576 or lower).

questions.question: What type of port does RSTP add that STP does not have?
questions.explanation: RSTP adds Alternate and Backup port roles. An Alternate port provides an alternative path to root (replaces blocking behavior) for faster failover.

questions.question: What Cisco STP mode implements RSTP per VLAN?
questions.explanation: Rapid PVST+ is Cisco's per-VLAN implementation of RSTP, combining the fast convergence of RSTP with per-VLAN spanning tree instances.

questions.question: What is the STP hello timer interval?
questions.explanation: The root bridge sends BPDUs every 2 seconds (hello timer). Non-root switches relay these BPDUs.

questions.question: A port is in err-disabled state due to BPDU Guard. How do you recover it?
questions.explanation: To recover a BPDU Guard err-disabled port, you must manually shut and no shut the interface. You can also enable automatic recovery with errdisable recovery cause bpduguard.

questions.question: In RSTP, what mechanism allows rapid transition to forwarding on point-to-point links?
questions.explanation: RSTP uses the Proposal/Agreement mechanism on point-to-point links to rapidly transition ports to forwarding without waiting for timers.

questions.question: What is the purpose of EtherChannel?
options: Bundling multiple physical links into one logical link
questions.explanation: EtherChannel bundles multiple physical links into a single logical link, providing increased bandwidth and redundancy without STP blocking.

questions.question: Which EtherChannel protocol is an IEEE standard?
questions.explanation: LACP (Link Aggregation Control Protocol) is the IEEE 802.3ad standard. PAgP is Cisco proprietary.

questions.question: What LACP mode actively tries to form an EtherChannel?
questions.explanation: LACP active mode actively sends LACP packets to negotiate an EtherChannel. Passive mode only responds. At least one side must be active.

questions.question: Which LACP mode combinations will form an EtherChannel?
questions.explanation: LACP requires at least one side to be Active. Active-Active and Active-Passive both form EtherChannels. Passive-Passive will not.

questions.question: What command assigns an interface to EtherChannel group 1 using LACP active mode?
questions.explanation: channel-group <number> mode active is the correct command to add an interface to an EtherChannel using LACP active negotiation.

questions.question: What must match for EtherChannel to form?
options: Speed, duplex, VLAN, and trunk settings
questions.explanation: All member interfaces must have matching speed, duplex, VLAN assignments, trunking mode, and allowed VLANs for EtherChannel to form.

questions.question: An EtherChannel shows (SD) flags in show etherchannel summary. What does this mean?
questions.explanation: S=Layer 2 (switched), D=Down. The EtherChannel has been created but is not functioning. Check for mismatched parameters between member interfaces.

questions.question: How many physical interfaces can be bundled into a single EtherChannel?
questions.explanation: Up to 8 active interfaces can be bundled. With LACP, you can configure up to 16 (8 active + 8 standby/hot-standby).

questions.question: What command shows the status of all EtherChannel bundles?
questions.explanation: show etherchannel summary provides a concise overview of all EtherChannel bundles, their status, protocol, and member ports.

questions.question: What is the 'on' mode for EtherChannel?
questions.explanation: The 'on' mode forces EtherChannel without LACP or PAgP negotiation. Both sides must be set to 'on'. No protocol packets are exchanged.

questions.question: What is the default native VLAN on a Cisco trunk?
questions.explanation: VLAN 1 is the default native VLAN. Frames from the native VLAN are sent untagged on 802.1Q trunks.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a trunk port?
options: Carry traffic from multiple VLANs between switches
questions.explanation: Trunk ports carry traffic for multiple VLANs between switches using 802.1Q tagging. Each frame is tagged with its VLAN ID except native VLAN.

questions.question: What command configures a port as a trunk?
questions.explanation: switchport mode trunk forces the interface into trunk mode. On some switches, you may also need switchport trunk encapsulation dot1q first.

questions.question: How do you change the native VLAN on a trunk to VLAN 99?
questions.explanation: switchport trunk native vlan <id> changes the native VLAN on a trunk port. Best practice is to use an unused VLAN.

questions.question: What command limits trunk traffic to VLANs 10, 20, and 30?
questions.explanation: switchport trunk allowed vlan defines which VLANs can traverse the trunk. Only specified VLANs will have their traffic carried.

questions.question: What is DTP?
options: Dynamic Trunk Protocol — negotiates trunk formation
questions.explanation: DTP (Dynamic Trunking Protocol) automatically negotiates whether a link becomes a trunk. Best practice is to disable it with switchport nonegotiate.

questions.question: Which DTP mode combinations form a trunk?
questions.explanation: Desirable-Desirable and Desirable-Auto both form trunks. Auto-Auto does NOT form a trunk — neither side actively negotiates.

questions.question: What happens if the native VLAN is mismatched on a trunk?
options: Frames may be forwarded to the wrong VLAN
questions.explanation: Native VLAN mismatch causes untagged frames to be placed in different VLANs on each end, creating a potential security risk (VLAN hopping).

questions.question: What is the 802.1Q tag size?
questions.explanation: The 802.1Q tag is 4 bytes inserted into the Ethernet frame: 2-byte TPID (0x8100) and 2-byte TCI containing priority, DEI, and 12-bit VLAN ID.

questions.question: What command verifies trunk configuration and active VLANs?
questions.explanation: show interfaces trunk displays trunk ports, their mode, encapsulation, native VLAN, and allowed/active VLANs.

questions.question: What is CDP?
options: Cisco Discovery Protocol — discovers directly connected Cisco devices
questions.explanation: CDP is a Cisco proprietary Layer 2 protocol that discovers directly connected Cisco devices and gathers information about them.

questions.question: What is LLDP and how does it differ from CDP?
options: LLDP is a faster version of CDP
LLDP is an IEEE standard; CDP is Cisco proprietary
LLDP works at Layer 3; CDP at Layer 2
questions.explanation: LLDP (Link Layer Discovery Protocol) is the IEEE 802.1AB standard equivalent to CDP. It works with multi-vendor equipment.

questions.question: What command shows CDP neighbor details?
questions.explanation: show cdp neighbors detail shows platform, IP address, capabilities, software version, and interface information for each CDP neighbor.

questions.question: How do you disable CDP on a specific interface?
questions.explanation: 'no cdp enable' disables CDP on a specific interface. 'no cdp run' disables CDP globally on the entire device.

questions.question: What information can CDP provide about a neighbor?
options: Device ID, IP address, platform, capabilities, interface
questions.explanation: CDP provides device ID, IP addresses, platform/model, capabilities (router/switch), local and remote interface names, software version, and native VLAN.

questions.question: What protocol does a Cisco lightweight AP use to communicate with a WLC?
questions.explanation: CAPWAP (Control and Provisioning of Wireless Access Points) is the protocol used between lightweight APs and Wireless LAN Controllers.

questions.question: What is the difference between autonomous and lightweight APs?
options: Lightweight APs require a WLC; autonomous APs operate independently
questions.explanation: Autonomous APs are self-contained with local configuration. Lightweight APs are managed centrally by a WLC and download their config from it.

questions.question: What AP mode handles normal client data traffic?
questions.explanation: Local mode is the default AP mode. The AP creates CAPWAP tunnels to the WLC for both management and client data traffic.

questions.question: What AP mode allows local switching of client traffic at a branch office?
questions.explanation: FlexConnect (formerly H-REAP) allows APs at remote sites to locally switch client data traffic even if the WLC connection is lost.

questions.question: How is a WLC typically connected to the network?
options: Via a trunk port carrying WLAN VLANs
Via an access port on VLAN 1
questions.explanation: A WLC connects to the network via a trunk port to carry traffic from multiple WLANs/VLANs. Often LAG is used for redundancy.

questions.question: What port type connects a lightweight AP to the switch?
questions.explanation: Lightweight APs connect to switch access ports. CAPWAP encapsulates all traffic in IP/UDP tunnels to the WLC, so no trunking is needed.

questions.question: Which management protocols can be used to access a WLC?
questions.explanation: WLCs support multiple management access methods: HTTP/HTTPS web GUI, SSH, console, and AAA via TACACS+/RADIUS.

questions.question: What is a WLAN in the context of a WLC?
options: A wireless network profile with SSID and security settings
questions.explanation: On a WLC, a WLAN is a configuration profile that defines the SSID, security method, QoS policy, and associated VLAN.

questions.question: What is the maximum number of WLANs typically supported on a Cisco WLC?
questions.explanation: Most Cisco WLCs support up to 512 WLANs, though only 16 can be active per AP radio at any time.

questions.question: Where do you configure WLAN security on a Cisco WLC?
options: The WLC GUI under WLANs section
questions.explanation: WLAN security settings (WPA2/WPA3, PSK/802.1X, etc.) are configured centrally on the WLC through the web GUI or CLI.

questions.question: What is a VLAN?
options: A logical network segment within a switch
questions.explanation: A VLAN (Virtual LAN) is a logical broadcast domain created within a switch. Devices in different VLANs cannot communicate without routing.

questions.question: What command creates VLAN 10 with the name 'Sales'?
questions.explanation: Enter VLAN configuration with 'vlan 10', then 'name Sales' on the next line. This is done from global config mode.

questions.question: What is the default VLAN on a Cisco switch?
questions.explanation: VLAN 1 is the default VLAN. All ports are in VLAN 1 by default. It cannot be deleted or renamed.

questions.question: How do you assign an interface to VLAN 10?
questions.explanation: Configure the interface, set switchport mode access, then switchport access vlan 10 to assign the port to VLAN 10.

questions.question: What is inter-VLAN routing?
options: Routing between different subnets within the same VLAN
questions.explanation: Inter-VLAN routing enables communication between devices in different VLANs. It requires a Layer 3 device (router or L3 switch).

questions.question: What is Router-on-a-Stick?
options: Inter-VLAN routing using a single router interface with subinterfaces
questions.explanation: Router-on-a-Stick uses a single physical router interface with 802.1Q subinterfaces to route between VLANs via a trunk link to the switch.

questions.question: What command creates a subinterface for VLAN 10 on Gi0/0?
questions.explanation: interface GigabitEthernet0/0.10 creates subinterface .10. Then configure encapsulation dot1Q 10 and an IP address for VLAN 10 routing.

questions.question: What command is required on a router subinterface for 802.1Q trunking?
questions.explanation: encapsulation dot1Q <vlan-id> tells the subinterface which VLAN's tagged traffic to accept and send. This is required for router-on-a-stick.

questions.question: What is an SVI (Switched Virtual Interface)?
options: A virtual interface on a Layer 3 switch representing a VLAN
questions.explanation: An SVI is a virtual Layer 3 interface on a switch associated with a VLAN. It provides the default gateway for that VLAN's hosts.

questions.question: What must be enabled on a Layer 3 switch for inter-VLAN routing via SVIs?
questions.explanation: The 'ip routing' command must be enabled on L3 switches to enable routing between SVIs. Without it, the switch only operates at Layer 2.

questions.question: What is the size of an IPv6 address?
questions.explanation: IPv6 addresses are 128 bits long (compared to 32 bits for IPv4), written as 8 groups of 4 hexadecimal digits.

questions.question: Which IPv6 address type is equivalent to a public IPv4 address?
questions.explanation: Global Unicast Addresses (GUA, 2000::/3) are routable on the internet, equivalent to public IPv4 addresses.

questions.question: What prefix identifies an IPv6 link-local address?
questions.explanation: Link-local addresses use the FE80::/10 prefix. They are automatically generated and only valid on the local link (not routable).

questions.question: What is SLAAC?
options: A method for hosts to auto-configure IPv6 addresses using Router Advertisements
questions.explanation: SLAAC (Stateless Address Autoconfiguration) allows hosts to generate their own IPv6 address using the prefix from Router Advertisements and an interface ID.

questions.question: What does NDP replace in IPv6 that ARP provides in IPv4?
questions.explanation: NDP (Neighbor Discovery Protocol) replaces ARP in IPv6. It uses ICMPv6 Neighbor Solicitation and Neighbor Advertisement messages.

questions.question: How does EUI-64 generate an interface ID from a MAC address?
options: Splits MAC in half, inserts FF:FE, flips 7th bit
questions.explanation: EUI-64: split MAC in half, insert FFFE in the middle, then flip the 7th bit (Universal/Local bit) of the first byte.

questions.question: What command enables IPv6 routing on a Cisco router?
questions.explanation: ipv6 unicast-routing enables IPv6 routing globally. Without it, the router processes IPv6 on interfaces but doesn't route between them.

questions.question: What command assigns an IPv6 address to an interface?
questions.explanation: ipv6 address is the correct interface command. Note IPv6 uses prefix length (/64) not a separate mask.

questions.question: What is the IPv6 multicast address for all nodes on the local link?
questions.explanation: FF02::1 is the all-nodes multicast address. FF02::2 is all-routers. FF02::5 is all-OSPF routers.

questions.question: What type of IPv6 address is FD00::1?
questions.explanation: FC00::/7 (practically FD00::/8) is the Unique Local Address range. These are similar to RFC 1918 private addresses in IPv4.

questions.question: How many bits are in the interface ID portion of a /64 IPv6 address?
questions.explanation: A /64 prefix means 64 bits for the network and 64 bits for the interface ID (host portion).

questions.question: Which IPv6 address type is automatically created on every IPv6-enabled interface?
questions.explanation: A link-local address (FE80::/10) is automatically generated whenever IPv6 is enabled on an interface. It's used for local communication and routing protocols.

questions.question: What does the :: notation represent in IPv6?
options: One or more groups of consecutive zeros
questions.explanation: The double colon :: represents one or more consecutive groups of all zeros. It can only appear once in an address to avoid ambiguity.

questions.question: What is the solicited-node multicast address used for?
questions.explanation: Solicited-node multicast (FF02::1:FFxx:xxxx) is used by NDP for efficient address resolution, targeting only the specific host instead of broadcasting.

questions.question: Which transport protocol provides reliable, ordered delivery?
questions.explanation: TCP provides reliable delivery through sequence numbers, acknowledgments, and retransmission. UDP is connectionless and unreliable.

questions.question: What is the TCP three-way handshake sequence?
questions.explanation: TCP establishes connections with SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK. This ensures both sides are ready to communicate.

questions.question: Which well-known port does HTTPS use?
questions.explanation: HTTPS uses TCP port 443. HTTP uses port 80. These are well-known ports assigned by IANA.

questions.question: Which protocol would you use for real-time voice/video traffic?
questions.explanation: UDP is preferred for real-time traffic because it has lower latency (no handshake, no retransmission). Some packet loss is acceptable for voice/video.

questions.question: What TCP port does SSH use?
questions.explanation: SSH uses TCP port 22. FTP uses 21, Telnet uses 23, and SMTP uses 25.

questions.question: How many usable host addresses are in a /26 subnet?
questions.explanation: A /26 has 6 host bits: 2^6 = 64 total addresses - 2 (network + broadcast) = 62 usable hosts.

questions.question: What is the subnet mask for a /20 network?
What is the network address for host 172.16.45.130/22?
questions.explanation: /22 has a block size of 4 in the third octet. 45/4 = 11 remainder 1, so network starts at 44. Network: 172.16.44.0

questions.question: What is the broadcast address for 192.168.1.0/25?
questions.explanation: /25 splits the last octet in half. First subnet: .0-.127. Network=.0, Broadcast=.127, Usable=.1-.126

questions.question: What is the wildcard mask for a /24 subnet?
Which of these is a private IPv4 address?
questions.explanation: 192.168.0.0/16 is a private range (RFC 1918). 10.0.0.0/8 and 172.16.0.0/12 are the other private ranges. 172.32.x is public.

questions.question: You need at least 500 host addresses per subnet. What is the minimum prefix length?
questions.explanation: /23 gives 510 hosts (2^9-2), which is just barely enough but /22 gives 1022 hosts providing more room. Actually /23=510 which is >=500, so /23 works. But wait: 2^9=512-2=510. So /23 is minimum.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a subnet mask?
options: To separate the network and host portions of an IP address
questions.explanation: A subnet mask identifies which bits of the IP address represent the network portion and which represent the host portion.

questions.question: What type of fiber optic cable supports longer distances?
questions.explanation: Single-mode fiber uses a smaller core (8-10 micron) with laser light, supporting distances up to 100km. Multimode uses LED and supports shorter distances.

questions.question: What is the maximum distance for Cat 5e/6 copper Ethernet?
questions.explanation: Cat 5e and Cat 6 UTP cables have a maximum distance of 100 meters (328 feet) for Ethernet at any speed up to 10 Gbps.

questions.question: A link shows CRC errors incrementing. What is the most likely cause?
questions.explanation: CRC errors typically indicate physical layer problems: damaged cable, loose connector, or electromagnetic interference.

questions.question: What symptom indicates a duplex mismatch?
options: High collision count and late collisions
questions.explanation: Duplex mismatch causes late collisions, FCS errors, and runts on the half-duplex side, and a poor-performing but 'up' link on the full-duplex side.

questions.question: What device operates at Layer 3 and makes forwarding decisions based on IP addresses?
questions.explanation: Routers operate at Layer 3 (Network layer) and use IP addresses in their routing table to forward packets between networks.

questions.question: What is the primary difference between a Layer 2 and Layer 3 switch?
options: Layer 3 switches can route between VLANs
questions.explanation: Layer 3 switches can perform routing (inter-VLAN routing via SVIs) in addition to switching. Layer 2 switches only forward based on MAC addresses.

questions.question: What is the advantage of a spine-leaf architecture?
questions.explanation: Spine-leaf provides consistent latency because every leaf switch is exactly one hop from every spine switch, creating equal-cost paths.

questions.question: What are the three non-overlapping channels for 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi?
questions.explanation: Channels 1, 6, and 11 are the three non-overlapping channels in the 2.4 GHz band. Using overlapping channels causes co-channel interference.

questions.question: What wireless encryption standard is considered most secure?
questions.explanation: WPA3 is the latest and most secure wireless encryption standard, using SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals) instead of the 4-way handshake.

questions.question: What is a hypervisor?
options: Software that creates and manages virtual machines
questions.explanation: A hypervisor (Type 1 or Type 2) is software that creates and manages virtual machines by abstracting hardware resources.

questions.question: What does a switch do when it receives a frame with an unknown destination MAC?
options: Sends it to the default gateway
Floods it out all ports except the source port
questions.explanation: When the destination MAC is not in the MAC address table, the switch floods the frame out all ports except the one it was received on.

questions.question: How does a switch learn MAC addresses?
options: By examining the source MAC of incoming frames
questions.explanation: Switches learn MAC addresses by recording the source MAC address and ingress port of each received frame into the MAC address table.

questions.question: What does NAT stand for?
questions.explanation: NAT (Network Address Translation) translates private IP addresses to public IP addresses for internet access.

questions.question: What is the difference between static NAT and dynamic NAT?
options: Static uses pools; dynamic uses one-to-one
Static maps one private to one public permanently; dynamic uses a pool
questions.explanation: Static NAT creates a permanent one-to-one mapping. Dynamic NAT uses a pool of public addresses assigned on a first-come basis.

questions.question: What is PAT (Port Address Translation)?
options: NAT overload — multiple private IPs share one public IP using port numbers
questions.explanation: PAT (also called NAT overload) allows many internal hosts to share a single public IP by using unique source port numbers to track sessions.

questions.question: What command defines the inside NAT interface?
questions.explanation: 'ip nat inside' is configured on the interface facing the private network. 'ip nat outside' goes on the internet-facing interface.

questions.question: What command configures PAT using the outside interface address?
options: ip nat inside source list 1 pool PAT overload
ip nat inside source list 1 interface Gi0/0 overload
questions.explanation: 'ip nat inside source list <acl> interface <outside-if> overload' configures PAT using the outside interface's IP address.
questions.ciscoCommand: ip nat inside source list 1 interface Gi0/0 overload

questions.question: What command verifies active NAT translations?
questions.explanation: show ip nat translations displays all current NAT translation entries, showing inside local/global and outside local/global addresses.

questions.question: What command configures a router as an NTP client?
questions.explanation: 'ntp server <ip>' configures the router to synchronize its clock with the specified NTP server.

questions.question: What is the NTP stratum value for the most accurate time source?
questions.explanation: Stratum 0 is the reference clock (atomic clock/GPS). Stratum 1 is directly connected to stratum 0 — these are the most accurate NTP servers.

questions.question: What port does DNS use?
questions.explanation: DNS uses UDP port 53 for queries and TCP port 53 for zone transfers or responses larger than 512 bytes.

questions.question: What is the role of a DHCP server?
options: Automatically assign IP configuration to clients
questions.explanation: DHCP automatically assigns IP address, subnet mask, default gateway, DNS servers, and other parameters to clients.

questions.question: What does SNMP stand for and what is it used for?
options: Simple Network Management Protocol — monitor and manage network devices
questions.explanation: SNMP allows network admins to monitor device health, performance metrics, and configuration from a central management station.

questions.question: What is an SNMP trap?
options: An unsolicited alert sent from an agent to the manager
A request from the manager to the agent
questions.explanation: SNMP traps are asynchronous notifications sent by SNMP agents to the NMS when a significant event occurs (link down, high CPU, etc.).

questions.question: What are syslog severity levels 0 and 7?
questions.explanation: Syslog levels: 0=Emergency, 1=Alert, 2=Critical, 3=Error, 4=Warning, 5=Notice, 6=Informational, 7=Debug. Lower number = more severe.

questions.question: What command sends syslog messages to a server at 10.0.0.100?
questions.explanation: 'logging <ip>' configures the device to send syslog messages to an external syslog server for centralized log collection.

questions.question: What is a DHCP relay agent?
options: A router that forwards DHCP broadcasts to a remote DHCP server
questions.explanation: A DHCP relay agent forwards DHCP broadcast requests from clients to a DHCP server on a different subnet using unicast.

questions.question: What command configures a DHCP relay on a router interface?
questions.explanation: ip helper-address forwards broadcast UDP traffic (including DHCP) to the specified server IP address.

questions.question: What are the three main QoS models?
questions.explanation: Best-effort (no QoS), IntServ (per-flow reservation with RSVP), and DiffServ (per-hop classification using DSCP) are the three QoS models.

questions.question: What is DSCP used for in QoS?
options: Marking packets with a priority value in the IP header
questions.explanation: DSCP (Differentiated Services Code Point) uses 6 bits in the IP header's ToS field to mark packets with QoS priority values (0-63).

questions.question: What DSCP value is used for Expedited Forwarding (voice traffic)?
questions.explanation: EF (Expedited Forwarding) uses DSCP 46. This is typically used for voice traffic requiring low latency, low jitter, and low loss.

questions.question: What is the difference between policing and shaping in QoS?
options: Policing buffers excess traffic; shaping drops it
Policing drops excess traffic; shaping buffers and delays it
Policing is for inbound; shaping is for outbound only
questions.explanation: Policing drops or re-marks traffic exceeding the rate limit immediately. Shaping buffers excess traffic and sends it when bandwidth is available.

questions.question: What command configures SSH version 2 on a Cisco device?
questions.explanation: ip ssh version 2 enables SSH version 2, which is more secure than version 1. You also need a hostname, domain name, and RSA keys.

questions.question: What are the prerequisites for configuring SSH on a Cisco router?
options: Hostname, domain name, RSA key pair, and VTY line configuration
questions.explanation: SSH requires: 1) hostname, 2) domain name, 3) RSA key pair (crypto key generate rsa), 4) VTY lines configured for SSH.

questions.question: What is the main difference between TFTP and FTP?
options: TFTP is simpler with no authentication; FTP supports login and directory browsing
FTP uses UDP; TFTP uses TCP
questions.explanation: TFTP uses UDP port 69 and provides no authentication. FTP uses TCP ports 20/21 with username/password authentication.

questions.question: What are the three modes of Cisco IOS CLI?
options: User EXEC, Privileged EXEC, Global Configuration
questions.explanation: User EXEC (>), Privileged EXEC (#), and Global Configuration (config)# are the three primary IOS modes. You move between them with enable and configure terminal.

questions.question: What command enters privileged EXEC mode?
questions.explanation: The 'enable' command moves from User EXEC mode (>) to Privileged EXEC mode (#), which provides full access to show and config commands.

questions.question: What command saves the running configuration to NVRAM?
questions.explanation: Both 'write memory' (shorthand) and 'copy running-config startup-config' save the active configuration to NVRAM for persistence across reboots.

questions.question: What does the 'enable secret' command do vs 'enable password'?
options: enable secret encrypts the password with MD5; enable password stores it in cleartext
enable secret is for SSH only
questions.explanation: enable secret stores the password as an MD5 hash and always takes precedence over enable password, which stores in cleartext or weak Type 7.

questions.question: What command encrypts all plaintext passwords in the running config?
questions.explanation: service password-encryption applies weak Type 7 encryption to all plaintext passwords in the config. It's not strong but better than plaintext.

questions.question: What is DHCP snooping?
options: A Layer 2 security feature that filters untrusted DHCP messages
questions.explanation: DHCP snooping validates DHCP messages from untrusted sources, building a binding table of MAC-to-IP mappings. It prevents rogue DHCP servers.

questions.question: What is Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI)?
options: A feature that validates ARP packets against the DHCP snooping binding table
questions.explanation: DAI intercepts ARP packets on untrusted ports and validates them against the DHCP snooping binding table to prevent ARP spoofing attacks.

questions.question: What happens when a port security violation occurs in shutdown mode?
options: The port sends a syslog message only
The port drops the violating frame
The port goes to err-disabled state
questions.explanation: In shutdown mode (default), the port is placed in err-disabled state when a violation occurs. This is the most secure but also most disruptive mode.

questions.question: What are the three port security violation modes?
questions.explanation: Protect silently drops violating traffic. Restrict drops and sends syslog. Shutdown puts the port in err-disabled state (default).

questions.question: What is sticky MAC learning in port security?
options: Dynamically learned MACs are added to the running config
MACs cannot be changed after learning
A type of static MAC assignment
questions.explanation: Sticky learning dynamically learns MAC addresses and adds them to the running-config as static entries, combining dynamic learning with persistence.

questions.question: What command enables DHCP snooping globally?
questions.explanation: ip dhcp snooping enables DHCP snooping globally. You must also enable it per VLAN with ip dhcp snooping vlan <id>.

questions.question: What is the difference between standard and extended ACLs?
options: Standard filters by destination; extended by source
Standard filters by source only; extended filters by source, destination, protocol, and port
Standard is numbered; extended is named
questions.explanation: Standard ACLs (1-99) match only source IP. Extended ACLs (100-199) match source IP, destination IP, protocol, and port numbers.

questions.question: Where should you place a standard ACL?
questions.explanation: Standard ACLs should be placed close to the destination because they only filter on source IP. Placing them at the source would block all traffic from that source.

questions.question: Where should you place an extended ACL?
questions.explanation: Extended ACLs should be placed close to the source because they can match specific traffic. This prevents unnecessary traffic from traversing the network.

questions.question: What does the 'host' keyword mean in an ACL?
questions.explanation: 'host 10.0.0.1' is shorthand for '10.0.0.1 0.0.0.0' — it matches exactly one IP address.

questions.question: What is at the end of every ACL?
questions.explanation: Every ACL has an invisible implicit deny all at the end. Any traffic not explicitly permitted by an ACE will be denied.

questions.question: What command applies ACL 100 inbound on interface Gi0/0?
questions.explanation: ip access-group <number/name> <in/out> applies an ACL to an interface in the specified direction (inbound or outbound).

questions.question: What is the difference between a site-to-site VPN and a remote access VPN?
options: Site-to-site uses SSL; remote access uses IPsec
Site-to-site connects networks; remote access connects individual users
questions.explanation: Site-to-site VPNs create encrypted tunnels between networks (office-to-office). Remote access VPNs connect individual users to a corporate network.

questions.question: What do the three As in AAA stand for?
questions.explanation: Authentication (who are you?), Authorization (what can you do?), and Accounting (what did you do?) form the AAA security framework.

questions.question: What is the difference between TACACS+ and RADIUS?
options: TACACS+ uses UDP; RADIUS uses TCP
TACACS+ encrypts the entire packet; RADIUS only encrypts the password
questions.explanation: TACACS+ (Cisco) uses TCP port 49 and encrypts the entire packet. RADIUS (open standard) uses UDP ports 1812/1813 and only encrypts the password field.

questions.question: What is the primary improvement of WPA3 over WPA2?
options: SAE replaces the 4-way handshake, preventing offline dictionary attacks
questions.explanation: WPA3 uses SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals) which provides protection against offline dictionary attacks and forward secrecy.

questions.question: What security method is commonly configured for enterprise wireless?
questions.explanation: WPA2-Enterprise uses 802.1X authentication with a RADIUS server, providing individual user credentials instead of a shared pre-shared key.

questions.question: What is the difference between a threat, vulnerability, and exploit?
options: Threat=potential danger, Vulnerability=weakness, Exploit=attack leveraging a vulnerability
questions.explanation: A threat is a potential danger, a vulnerability is a weakness in a system, and an exploit is the method used to take advantage of a vulnerability.

questions.question: What is the difference between the control plane and data plane?
options: Control plane forwards packets; data plane makes routing decisions
Control plane makes routing decisions; data plane forwards packets
Control plane is hardware; data plane is software
questions.explanation: The control plane runs routing protocols and builds the routing/forwarding tables. The data plane uses those tables to forward actual traffic.

questions.question: What are Northbound and Southbound APIs in SDN?
options: NB connects to users; SB connects to servers
NB connects apps to the controller; SB connects the controller to network devices
NB is for monitoring; SB is for configuration
questions.explanation: Northbound APIs expose controller functionality to applications. Southbound APIs (like OpenFlow, NETCONF) communicate with network devices.

questions.question: What is Cisco DNA Center?
options: A centralized network management and automation platform
questions.explanation: Cisco DNA Center is an intent-based networking controller that provides centralized management, automation, assurance, and security for campus networks.

questions.question: What is intent-based networking?
options: Expressing business intent and having the network translate it to device configurations
Using AI to replace network engineers
questions.explanation: Intent-based networking allows admins to express what they want (intent), and the controller translates that into device-level configurations and validates compliance.

questions.question: How does controller-based networking differ from traditional networking?
options: A central controller manages the control plane; traditional uses distributed control on each device
questions.explanation: In controller-based networking, the control plane is centralized on a controller (like DNA Center), while traditional networking has distributed control on each device.

questions.question: What HTTP method is used to create a new resource in a REST API?
questions.explanation: POST creates a new resource. GET retrieves, PUT updates/replaces, PATCH partially updates, and DELETE removes a resource.

questions.question: What does CRUD stand for in REST APIs?
questions.explanation: CRUD maps to HTTP methods: Create=POST, Read=GET, Update=PUT/PATCH, Delete=DELETE.

questions.question: Which configuration management tool uses playbooks written in YAML?
questions.explanation: Ansible uses YAML playbooks for configuration management. It is agentless (uses SSH) unlike Puppet (uses agents) and Chef (uses agents).

questions.question: What is the key advantage of Ansible over Puppet for network devices?
options: Ansible is agentless — uses SSH, no software needed on managed devices
questions.explanation: Ansible is agentless, connecting via SSH. This is ideal for network devices that can't run agents. Puppet and Chef require agents installed on managed nodes.

questions.question: In JSON, what symbol encloses an object?
questions.explanation: JSON objects are enclosed in curly braces {}. Arrays use square brackets []. Keys are strings in double quotes followed by colon and value.

questions.question:
A network engineer runs 'show interfaces gi0/1' and sees the following output:

GigabitEthernet0/1 is up, line protocol is up
  Hardware is Gigabit Ethernet, address is 0023.33aa.bb01
  Full-duplex, 1000Mb/s
  1247 CRC errors, 0 collisions, 89 giants, 0 runts

Which is the MOST LIKELY cause of the CRC errors?
options: A duplex mismatch between this port and the connected device
A bad cable, damaged SFP, or Layer 1 physical issue
The connected device is sending frames larger than the MTU
The switch port is running at the wrong speed
questions.explanation: CRC errors with full-duplex and 0 collisions point to a Layer 1 physical problem — bad cable, damaged connector, or faulty SFP. A) Duplex mismatch would produce late collisions on the half-duplex side, not CRC errors with 0 collisions. C) Frames larger than MTU cause giants, which are also present but giants alone don't cause CRC errors — CRC errors mean the frame check sequence failed, indicating signal corruption. D) Speed mismatch would typically cause the interface to go down or show constant errors, not just CRC errors.

questions.question: A router's GigabitEthernet0/0 interface shows 'up/up' but the connected switch port shows 'up/down'. What is the MOST LIKELY explanation?
options: There is a Layer 1 problem with the cable between the devices
The switch port has been administratively shut down
This scenario is impossible — if one side is up/up the other cannot be up/down
STP is blocking the switch port
questions.explanation: This scenario is indeed impossible under normal operation. If the physical layer (line) is up on the router side, the switch must also see link on that port. B) An administratively shut port would show 'administratively down/down', not 'up/down'. C) This is the correct answer — one side cannot be up/up while the other side's line protocol is down on a direct connection. D) STP blocking does not change the interface status to up/down; a blocked port still shows up/up. The question tests whether you recognize an impossible state in show output.

questions.question: A company has a collapsed core/distribution design using two Layer 3 switches. Each access switch has a single uplink to one of the two core switches. Inter-VLAN routing is handled by the core switches. If Core Switch A fails, what happens to hosts on access switches connected ONLY to Core Switch A?
options: They lose inter-VLAN routing but can still communicate within their local VLAN
They lose all network connectivity since their only uplink is down
STP reconverges and traffic is rerouted through Core Switch B
HSRP or VRRP automatically redirects traffic to Core Switch B
questions.explanation: With only a single uplink to Core Switch A, if that switch fails the physical path is broken entirely. B) This is correct — the access switch has no alternate physical path. There is no redundant link to Core Switch B. C) STP cannot reroute traffic because there is no alternate physical link — STP works with redundant links, not missing links. D) HSRP/VRRP provide gateway redundancy but require the access switch to have a path to the backup gateway, which it does not. Even local VLAN traffic on the same access switch would still work between ports on that switch, making A partially true but misleading — the question asks about hosts that need to reach the core.

questions.question: In a three-tier campus architecture, which layer is PRIMARILY responsible for applying network access policies and QoS marking?
options: Both the core and distribution layers share this responsibility
questions.explanation: The distribution layer is the policy enforcement point in a three-tier design. It handles access control lists, QoS policy marking, route summarization, and inter-VLAN routing. A) The core layer should be kept fast and simple — no policy enforcement, no ACLs, no QoS marking. C) While QoS trust boundaries may start at the access layer, the primary policy enforcement and marking decisions are at distribution. D) The core layer should never apply policies — that adds latency to the high-speed backbone.

questions.question:
A switch has the following interface configured:

interface GigabitEthernet0/1
  switchport mode trunk
  switchport trunk native vlan 99
  switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,20,30

A frame arrives tagged with VLAN 99. What does the switch do?
options: It forwards the frame normally on VLAN 99
It drops the frame because VLAN 99 is not in the allowed VLAN list
It forwards the frame as untagged on the trunk since 99 is the native VLAN
It strips the tag and forwards on VLAN 99 after adding it to the allowed list automatically
questions.explanation: The allowed VLAN list on the trunk is 10, 20, and 30. VLAN 99 is NOT in the allowed list. Even though VLAN 99 is configured as the native VLAN, it is still filtered by the allowed VLAN list. The frame is dropped. A) VLAN 99 would need to be in the allowed list to be forwarded. C) This would be true if VLAN 99 were in the allowed list, but it is explicitly excluded. D) Cisco switches do not auto-add VLANs to the allowed list. This is a common misconfiguration — admins set a native VLAN but forget to add it to the allowed VLAN list.

questions.question: An administrator sees port 443 traffic blocked by a firewall but port 80 traffic is allowed. Users can browse http://example.com but not https://example.com. Which protocol and transport layer details explain this?
options: HTTP uses TCP/80 and HTTPS uses UDP/443
HTTP uses TCP/80 and HTTPS uses TCP/443
HTTP uses UDP/80 and HTTPS uses TCP/443
HTTP uses TCP/80 and HTTPS uses TCP/443, but HTTPS also requires UDP/443 for the TLS handshake
questions.explanation: HTTP uses TCP port 80 and HTTPS uses TCP port 443. Both are TCP-based. A) HTTPS does not use UDP/443 — it runs TLS over TCP. C) HTTP uses TCP, not UDP. D) The TLS handshake occurs entirely over TCP/443 — there is no UDP requirement for HTTPS. The newer QUIC/HTTP3 protocol does use UDP/443, but standard HTTPS does not, and the CCNA exam tests standard HTTPS.

questions.question: Which of the following protocols uses the SAME port number for BOTH TCP and UDP?
questions.explanation: The question asks which protocols use the SAME port for both TCP and UDP. DNS uses port 53 for both TCP (zone transfers, large responses) and UDP (standard queries). Syslog uses port 514 for both UDP (traditional) and TCP (reliable syslog per RFC 3195). A) TFTP is UDP-only (port 69), and FTP data (port 20) is TCP-only. B) DNS uses both TCP/UDP on 53, but SNMP uses only UDP on 161/162. D) DNS qualifies, but NTP uses only UDP on port 123. The correct pair where BOTH protocols individually use the same port for TCP and UDP is DNS and Syslog.

questions.question: A network admin configures an ACL to block Telnet but allow SSH. Which port numbers should be specified in the ACL?
questions.explanation: Telnet uses TCP port 23 and SSH uses TCP port 22. Both are TCP-based. B) This is correct. A) This reverses the port numbers — blocking 22 would block SSH and permitting 23 would permit Telnet, the opposite of what's desired. C) SSH uses TCP, not UDP. D) Telnet uses TCP, not UDP.

questions.question: A user reports that SFTP file transfers are being blocked. The firewall admin checks the rules and sees that TCP port 21 is permitted. Why are SFTP transfers still failing?
options: SFTP requires both TCP port 21 and a range of high ports for data transfer
SFTP uses TCP port 22, not port 21 — it runs over SSH, not FTP
SFTP uses TCP port 990 for implicit TLS connections
SFTP requires UDP port 69 which is being blocked
questions.explanation: SFTP (SSH File Transfer Protocol) runs over SSH on TCP port 22. It is NOT related to FTP despite the similar name. A) This describes FTP (active mode), not SFTP. Port 21 is FTP control. C) Port 990 is FTPS (FTP over implicit TLS), which is also different from SFTP. D) UDP port 69 is TFTP, a completely different protocol. This is one of the most common confusion points: SFTP != FTP over SSL. SFTP = file transfer over SSH (port 22). FTPS = FTP over TLS (port 990 implicit or 21 explicit).

questions.question: A host has the IP address 172.16.45.200/21. What is the network address and broadcast address of this subnet?
questions.explanation: /21 means 255.255.248.0. The block size in the third octet is 256-248=8. To find the network, divide 45 by 8: 45/8 = 5 remainder 5, so the network starts at 5*8=40. Network: 172.16.40.0, broadcast: 172.16.47.255. B) This assumes /24, not /21. C) 44 is not a multiple of the block size 8. D) This would be /19 (block size 32).

questions.question: You need to create exactly 14 subnets from the 10.0.0.0/8 network, each supporting at least 1000 hosts. Which subnet mask should you use?
questions.explanation: You need at least 14 subnets with 1000+ hosts each. /22 gives 2^14 = 16384 subnets from a /8 (borrowing 14 bits), but that's wrong — let's think from /8. From /8 to /22 borrows 14 host bits for subnets = 2^14 subnets, each with 2^10-2 = 1022 hosts. That satisfies both requirements. A) /18 from /8 borrows 10 bits = 1024 subnets, each with 2^14-2 = 16382 hosts — wastes massive address space. B) /20 from /8 borrows 12 bits = 4096 subnets, each with 2^12-2 = 4094 hosts — also works but wastes more than /22. D) /12 from /8 borrows 4 bits = 16 subnets with 2^20-2 = 1048574 hosts — enough subnets but grossly wasteful. The BEST answer is /22: it provides enough subnets (16384 > 14) and enough hosts (1022 > 1000) with the most efficient use of address space.

questions.question: Which of the following IP addresses is in the SAME subnet as 192.168.100.77/27?
questions.explanation: /27 has a block size of 32. 77/32 = 2 remainder 13, so the subnet starts at 64 and the broadcast is 95. The valid range is 192.168.100.64-95. A) 95 is the broadcast address of this subnet — it is IN the subnet (the question asks same subnet, not valid host). B) 96 is the first address of the NEXT subnet (96-127). C) 63 is in the previous subnet (32-63). D) 65 is in the same subnet (64-95). Both A and D are in the same subnet, but 95 is the broadcast — still part of the subnet. Since both A and D qualify, but D is a valid host address, the trap is recognizing that 95 IS in the subnet even though it's the broadcast.

questions.question: A network has the address 10.10.128.0/17. How many usable host addresses are available?
questions.explanation: /17 leaves 15 host bits. 2^15 = 32768 total addresses. Subtract 2 for network and broadcast = 32766 usable hosts. B) 32768 includes network and broadcast — not usable. C) 65534 would be /16, not /17. D) 65536 would be /16 without subtracting network and broadcast.

questions.question: An ISP assigns you 203.0.113.0/24. You need 5 subnets: one with 100 hosts, one with 50, one with 25, one with 10, and one with 2 (point-to-point link). Using VLSM, what is the subnet mask for the 100-host subnet?
questions.explanation: With VLSM, allocate the largest subnet first. 100 hosts requires at least 7 host bits (2^7-2=126 usable). That is a /25 (128 addresses). A) /25 gives 126 usable hosts — this is the smallest mask that fits 100 hosts. B) /26 gives only 62 usable hosts — not enough for 100. C) /24 gives 254 hosts — works but wastes the entire remaining address space for one subnet. D) /27 gives only 30 usable hosts — far too small.

questions.question: What is the valid host range for the subnet 172.16.16.0/22?
questions.explanation: /22 has a block size of 4 in the third octet (255.255.252.0). Network: 172.16.16.0, broadcast: 172.16.19.255. Valid host range is 172.16.16.1 to 172.16.19.254. B) This assumes /24. C) This would be /20 (block size 16). D) This includes the network address and broadcast address, which are not valid host addresses.

questions.question: A router has interface Gi0/0 with IP 10.1.1.1/30 and interface Gi0/1 with IP 10.1.1.5/30. A packet destined for 10.1.1.7 arrives on Gi0/0. What does the router do?
options: Routes the packet out Gi0/1 because 10.1.1.7 is on the 10.1.1.4/30 subnet
Drops the packet because 10.1.1.7 is a broadcast address, and routers do not forward broadcasts
Routes the packet out Gi0/0 because both addresses are in the 10.1.1.0/24 network
Sends an ICMP redirect back to the source
questions.explanation: 10.1.1.4/30 has network 10.1.1.4, valid hosts 10.1.1.5-6, and broadcast 10.1.1.7. The destination 10.1.1.7 is a directed broadcast for the 10.1.1.4/30 subnet. By default, Cisco routers do NOT forward directed broadcasts (ip directed-broadcast is disabled by default since IOS 12.0). The router drops it. A) While 10.1.1.7 is on the Gi0/1 subnet, it's the broadcast address — the router won't forward it by default. C) The router uses longest-prefix matching (/30), not /24. D) ICMP redirects are for when a better path exists on the same segment the packet arrived on.

questions.question: Which of the following IP addresses is a private address that could appear on an internal corporate network?
questions.explanation: RFC 1918 private ranges are: 10.0.0.0/8 (10.0.0.0-10.255.255.255), 172.16.0.0/12 (172.16.0.0-172.31.255.255), 192.168.0.0/16 (192.168.0.0-192.168.255.255). C) 10.255.255.1 falls within 10.0.0.0/8 — private. A) 172.32.1.1 is outside the 172.16-31 range — it's public. B) 192.169.1.1 is outside 192.168.x.x — it's public. D) 169.254.x.x is APIPA (link-local), not RFC 1918 private. It's used for auto-configuration when DHCP fails.

questions.question:
A router performing NAT has the following translation entry:

Pro  Inside global     Inside local      Outside local     Outside global
tcp  203.0.113.5:1024  192.168.1.10:4567 93.184.216.34:80  93.184.216.34:80

A packet arrives from the internet with destination 203.0.113.5:1024. Which address does the router translate the destination to?
questions.explanation: For return traffic from the internet, the router translates the Inside Global address (203.0.113.5:1024) back to the Inside Local address (192.168.1.10:4567). This is PAT (Port Address Translation) in action. A) This is the outside address — the router doesn't translate the destination to the outside address for inbound traffic. C) The port is also translated — the original internal port was 4567, not 1024. D) This mixes the global IP with the local port.

questions.question: Which of the following is a valid shortened form of the IPv6 address 2001:0db8:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0001?
options: All of the above are valid
questions.explanation: All three representations are valid. A) Uses :: to replace the longest consecutive run of all-zero groups — this is the most compressed form and valid. B) Removes leading zeros from each group but does not use :: — valid. C) Uses :: to replace some zero groups but explicitly writes others — valid, though not the most efficient. IPv6 allows any valid shortening as long as :: appears at most once. All three resolve to the same address.

questions.question: Which of the following IPv6 addresses is INVALID?
questions.explanation: A) INVALID — the :: can only appear ONCE in an IPv6 address. Using it twice creates ambiguity because the parser cannot determine how many groups each :: represents. B) Valid — this is a link-local address with a zone ID (%eth0), used to specify the interface. C) Valid — this is an IPv4-mapped IPv6 address (::ffff: prefix). D) Valid — :: replaces a single group of zeros (0000), and the total is 8 groups.

questions.question: An interface is configured with the IPv6 address 2001:db8:1::1/64. Which IPv6 address is automatically present on this interface WITHOUT any additional configuration?
options: A link-local address derived from the interface's MAC address or randomly generated
questions.explanation: When any IPv6 address is configured on an interface, a link-local address (fe80::/10) is automatically generated using either EUI-64 (from the MAC address) or a random identifier, depending on the OS. The interface also joins solicited-node multicast groups. A) ff02::1 is a multicast group the interface joins (listens to), not an address assigned to the interface. B) The link-local won't be exactly fe80::1 unless manually configured — it's derived from the MAC or randomly generated. D) ::1 is only on the loopback interface, not on physical interfaces.

questions.question: A host sends a packet to ff02::1:ff00:1. What type of address is this and what is its purpose?
options: All-nodes multicast — reaches every IPv6 node on the link
All-routers multicast — reaches every router on the link
Solicited-node multicast — used for Neighbor Discovery to find a specific host's MAC address
Anycast — reaches the nearest router advertising that prefix
questions.explanation: ff02::1:ff00:1 is a solicited-node multicast address (ff02::1:ff + last 24 bits of the unicast address). It is used by Neighbor Discovery Protocol (NDP) instead of ARP. When a host needs to resolve an IPv6 address to a MAC, it sends a Neighbor Solicitation to the solicited-node multicast address. A) All-nodes is ff02::1, not ff02::1:ff00:1. B) All-routers is ff02::2. D) Anycast addresses are indistinguishable from unicast in format.

questions.question: Which statement about IPv6 SLAAC (Stateless Address Autoconfiguration) is TRUE?
options: SLAAC requires a DHCPv6 server to provide the default gateway
SLAAC uses Router Advertisement messages to provide the prefix and default gateway
SLAAC provides DNS server addresses in the Router Advertisement by default on Cisco routers
SLAAC cannot be used with DHCPv6 — they are mutually exclusive
questions.explanation: SLAAC uses Router Advertisements (RA) from ICMPv6 to provide the network prefix, prefix length, and default gateway. The host generates its own interface ID. A) SLAAC does NOT need DHCPv6 for the gateway — the RA message includes the router's link-local address as the default gateway. C) By default, Cisco routers do not include DNS info in RAs. RDNSS (RFC 8106) can add DNS to RAs, but it's not default. Typically, DNS is provided by stateless DHCPv6. D) SLAAC can work alongside stateless DHCPv6 (for DNS, etc.) — they are complementary, not exclusive.

questions.question: A router advertises a /64 prefix via SLAAC with the M flag set to 0 and the O flag set to 1. What behavior will a properly configured IPv6 host exhibit?
options: The host ignores SLAAC and obtains its address entirely from DHCPv6
The host uses SLAAC for its address and prefix, then contacts a DHCPv6 server for other information like DNS
The host uses SLAAC for the prefix and DHCPv6 for the interface ID portion of the address
The host ignores the RA entirely because the flags conflict
questions.explanation: M=0 means 'do not use DHCPv6 for address assignment' and O=1 means 'use DHCPv6 for other configuration' (DNS, NTP, etc.). The host builds its own address via SLAAC and contacts a stateless DHCPv6 server for additional parameters. A) M=0 means don't get address from DHCPv6. C) The interface ID is always self-generated in SLAAC (EUI-64 or random) — DHCPv6 never provides just the interface ID. D) M=0, O=1 is a valid and common combination.

questions.question: Which verification step confirms that a Cisco switch has been assigned an IP address for remote management?
options: show ip interface brief — check the VLAN interface status and IP
show interfaces — check the physical port IP assignments
show ip route — verify the management subnet is in the routing table
show running-config — look for ip address under each physical interface
questions.explanation: On a Layer 2 switch, the management IP is assigned to a VLAN interface (SVI), typically VLAN 1 or a dedicated management VLAN. 'show ip interface brief' shows all interfaces including SVIs with their IP addresses and status. B) Physical switch ports don't have IP addresses in Layer 2 mode. C) show ip route works on Layer 3 switches but on a basic L2 switch the routing table is minimal. D) While running-config shows the configuration, it's not a verification of operational status — the SVI could be configured but down.

questions.question: A PC at 192.168.1.50/24 with default gateway 192.168.1.1 attempts to reach 10.1.1.100. Pinging 192.168.1.1 succeeds. Pinging 10.1.1.100 fails. The router at 192.168.1.1 has a route to 10.1.1.0/24. What should you check NEXT?
options: Verify that the return route exists on the remote network back to 192.168.1.0/24
Check if the PC has the correct subnet mask
Verify that the PC's default gateway MAC address is in its ARP table
Check if ICMP is being blocked by a firewall on the PC
questions.explanation: The PC can reach its gateway (ping succeeds), so the local network and gateway are fine. The router has a route to the destination. The most likely issue is asymmetric routing — the remote device at 10.1.1.100 receives the ping but has no route back to 192.168.1.0/24 (or its gateway doesn't). Always check the return path. B) The subnet mask is likely correct since local communication works. C) The gateway MAC is resolved since pings to 192.168.1.1 work. D) Possible but unlikely — firewall on the PC would typically block incoming, not outgoing ICMP.

questions.question:
A router shows the following output:

Router# show ip route
S    10.0.0.0/8 [1/0] via 192.168.1.2
C    192.168.1.0/24 is directly connected, GigabitEthernet0/0

A packet arrives for destination 10.5.5.5. The router sends it to 192.168.1.2, but that next-hop router has no route for 10.5.5.5. What does the next-hop router do?
options: Forwards the packet using its default route if one exists
Drops the packet and sends an ICMP Destination Unreachable back to the source
Sends the packet back to the first router
questions.explanation: When a router has no matching route in its routing table and no default route, it drops the packet and sends an ICMP Destination Unreachable (Type 3, Code 0 — Network Unreachable) back to the source. A) Only if a default route exists — the question states there is no route. B) Routers don't drop silently — they send ICMP unreachable by default (unless 'no ip unreachables' is configured). D) Sending it back would create a loop. The router doesn't know where to forward it, so it drops and notifies.

questions.question:
A network admin runs 'traceroute 10.1.1.1' from a router and sees:

1  192.168.1.2  4ms  3ms  4ms
2  * * *
3  10.1.1.1  8ms  7ms  8ms

What is the MOST LIKELY reason for the * * * at hop 2?
options: The router at hop 2 is down or unreachable
The router at hop 2 has an ACL blocking ICMP Time Exceeded messages or is configured with 'no ip unreachables'
There is packet loss between hop 1 and hop 3
The TTL value was set too low for the traceroute
questions.explanation: Since hop 3 responds successfully, the path is clearly working. The * * * means the router at hop 2 is not sending ICMP Time Exceeded replies.
